I'm Di Landy from Man Cordero and thank you for the opportunity to make the submission.

This bill appears at first glance to be a reasonable update to the 1966 Anzac Amendment, while allowing for commemoration of other New Zealand servicemen and women who have died in the line of duty since the Anzacs first landed act oli. But on closer examination, it contains wording what suggests that the goal of this bill is not so much to honor our fallen, but to rewrite the origin story of our nationhood and of our sister nation relationship with Australians.

The sacrifice and courage of the anzacs and the story tells us something about ourselves, who we were beginning to become here all together in these southern islands and who we remain and want to continue to strive to be, we are reminded by the actions that we can be brave, willing, resourceful, and good humor under extreme pressure, and that we share a common character and can unite the authors of this bill, say they're desirous of being more inclusive.

And to borrow one of the phrases so favored by inclusivity, advocates intend to decenter the anzacs themselves and all of our best qualities to make way for apparently everybody who's ever heard of New Zealand. The bill is more overreach from this government for a misguided purpose of the taxpayer dime.

Our history has been rapidly o overwritten, like so much outdated code, and nobody seems to see the cost to all of us financial and otherwise. I recognize that veterans affairs and others have consulted on this bill, and yet the details leave me wondering if this consultation, like so many others in recent years was held only with the top brass and left out the veterans themselves or indeed of the purpose of the extra inclusivity or spelled out to anyone.

I would like Anzac Day to stay as Anzac Day and for this bill to be discarded as unnecessary or altered significantly. The services and commemorations as currently conducted acknowledge the sacrifice of New Zealand Defense Force members from other conflicts. And there is no reason why an additional timeline cannot be included that continues this acknowledgement.

However, there is equally no reason to list everyone who has ever been tangentially involved with the New Zealand Defense Force and very good reason not to. Other countries already commemorate and honor their dead. And we recognize and uphold these relationships and memories in any number of ways.

But Anzac today is for us and for Australians. We need our history and our stories to tell the truth. Not to change with fashionable, but partisan and short-lived ideas about inclusivity. World War I and the Anzacs also remain an important part of global history and how our, how we are perceived internationally.

Many things were initiated from World War I that are still current and relevant today, are we to simply retell our story to the world and nevermind what the cost to their knowledge of us and our role in the world will be. What kind of diplomacy and inter international relations is this?

Why must this bill include every ward to date and those yet to happen? Don't you think this is a bit concerning? Not only is the bill attempting to reframe our history and minimize our founding unified moment, but it seeks to control it into the future. Towards that have not even happened yet. As artificial intelligence is increasingly used to write histories and essays, our stories will be recorded with this new slant.

For always in practice. This treasured commemoration for New Zealand in Australia will become nothing more than empty participation awards, especially considering the wards yet to happen part. I would also like to see a more coherent and specific definition of war-like conflict.

If the bill succeeds, the government will once again be telling people what to think. On Anzac Day of all days, people who go to Anzac Dawn Services whose whanau have or are still doing tours of duty, are already thinking of their loved ones, their whanau, and first and foremost in their minds and prayers.

They do not need to be reminded in this way to be inclusive or to not forget that others from other places have died too. We are entitled to a day to remember our own, and that day has been part of my life since I was a little girl. It is stable, consistent, and meaningful. And now the government wants to change it to be inclusive by reducing the role of the anzacs.
